target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): add longDescription to plugin approval payload
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `plugin.approval.request` caps `description` at 256 chars, so HTML-capable channels (Telegram, Discord, Matrix, Mattermost) truncate verdicts, JSON payloads, and action footers mid-sentence even though those surfaces could carry several thousand chars per message. Approvers see incomplete context and have to deny-and-retry.
- Why it matters: complex plugin approvals (file rewrites, command audits, multi-step verdicts) are exactly the case where reviewers need the full payload. Raising the global cap is wrong because SMS-class channels have real payload constraints; we need a per-payload opt-in.
- What changed: added an optional `longDescription` field (max 4000 chars, schema-validated) to `PluginApprovalRequestPayload` plus a small helper, `resolvePluginApprovalLongDescription`, that returns `longDescription` when non-blank and otherwise falls back to `description`. Default SDK text fallback (`buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage`), `PluginApprovalViewBase`, the Discord approval container, and the Matrix approval metadata / reconstructed pending payload all flow through that helper, so Telegram, Discord, and Matrix render the long form when it is provided and behave exactly as before when it is not.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no public type was renamed or removed, no existing schema field was relaxed, exec approvals were not touched, the Slack approval handler still only renders exec approvals (`eventKinds: ["exec"]`), and the secondary `deliverPrompt?: boolean` feature from the issue is intentionally deferred — it is a separate lifecycle change that warrants its own PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #81901
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

- Behavior or issue addressed: `plugin.approval.request` now accepts `longDescription` (≤4000 chars). When a plugin sends one, channels that already render the SDK pending payload (Telegram), the Discord plugin-approval container, and the Matrix custom event metadata all surface the full long-form context; channels that don't supply `longDescription` see exactly the same 256-char `description` body they used before.
- Real environment tested: Repo-local Vitest harness (Node 20.19 against the production code paths the SDK and bundled channel runtimes import). The provider HTTP boundary is mocked at the channel layer — the assertion the new tests make is that the SDK pending text body contains the long form verbatim and *not* the short summary, which is the exact link any channel renderer (Telegram, Matrix, fallback-text consumers) reads.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  PATH="/home/administrator/.nvm/versions/node/v20.19.0/bin:$PATH" \
    pnpm test \
      src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts \
      src/infra/plugin-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
      src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts \
      extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
      extensions/discord/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts
  PATH="/home/administrator/.nvm/versions/node/v20.19.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
  PATH="/home/administrator/.nvm/versions/node/v20.19.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
    src/infra/plugin-approvals.ts \
    src/infra/approval-view-model.ts \
    src/infra/approval-view-model.types.ts \
    src/gateway/protocol/schema/plugin-approvals.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts \
    extensions/discord/src/approval-handler.runtime.ts \
    extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.ts \
    CHANGELOG.md
  ```
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
  ```
  RUN  v4.1.6 /home/administrator/Desktop/Work/openclaw
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  7 passed (7)              # src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts (5 existing + 2 new)
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests 13 passed (13)              # src/infra/plugin-approval-forwarder.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests 25 passed (25)              # src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  7 passed (7)              # extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  1 passed (1)              # extensions/discord/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts
  [test] passed 5 Vitest shards
  
  OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256
  
  Checking formatting...
  All matched files use the correct format.
  Finished in 2907ms on 9 files using 1 threads.
  ```
- Observed result after fix: SDK pending text contains the full `longDescription` content verbatim; with `longDescription` blank/whitespace the test asserts the short `description` is still rendered. The plugin-sdk API baseline check accepts the new exports (`PLUGIN_APPROVAL_LONG_DESCRIPTION_MAX_LENGTH`, `resolvePluginApprovalLongDescription`, plus the existing-cap re-exports).
- What was not tested: I did not exercise a live Telegram / Discord / Matrix tenant with a real 4000-char approval payload. The unit tests cover the message-building path each channel renderer feeds into; a live channel smoke would prove the upstream platform actually renders the longer body, but it cannot reproduce the SDK-side decision the tests cover.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): With the new test temporarily flipped (`expect(payload.text).toContain("Short summary that should be replaced by longDescription.")`) it fails — the SDK body now starts with the long form, which is precisely the behavior change this PR introduces.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition. The historical 256-char cap predates HTML-capable channels having modern payload limits (Telegram 4096, Discord 2000+, Slack 4000+, Matrix MSC); it was correct for SMS-class surfaces and is still correct as the *short* description. The cap was simply the only knob plugins had.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts` — two new cases co-located with the existing `buildPluginApprovalPendingReplyPayload` coverage.
- Scenario the test should lock in: (1) when `longDescription` is non-blank, the SDK pending text body must contain it verbatim and must NOT contain the short `description`. (2) when `longDescription` is whitespace-only, the text must still contain the original `description` — proves the trim-aware fallback.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: every HTML-capable channel that doesn't render its own custom plugin-approval body (Telegram, Matrix's reply payload, any future channel) reads `buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage`. Locking that one function is the smallest unit that covers all those surfaces in one go.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): No pre-existing case asserted long-form preference or blank fallback; previous assertions only ran the default `description` path.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — two new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

For plugins:

- `plugin.approval.request` now accepts an optional `longDescription` field (1–4000 chars). The existing `description` field (1–256 chars) is unchanged and still required.
- New SDK exports through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime`:
  - `PLUGIN_APPROVAL_LONG_DESCRIPTION_MAX_LENGTH` (= 4000)
  - `PLUGIN_APPROVAL_DESCRIPTION_MAX_LENGTH` (already existed internally; now publicly re-exported)
  - `PLUGIN_APPROVAL_TITLE_MAX_LENGTH` (same)
  - `resolvePluginApprovalLongDescription(payload)` — returns `longDescription` when non-blank, else `description`.

For approvers (humans):

- When a plugin author supplies `longDescription`, Telegram / Discord / Matrix surfaces render the full long-form context (within the channel's own platform cap) instead of the truncated 256-char `description`.
- Plugins that do not opt in see identical rendering to before.

No config keys added or removed. No defaults flipped.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
plugin -> plugin.approval.request({ description: "256-char max..." })
  -> PluginApprovalRequestPayload.description
       -> buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage uses description verbatim
            -> Telegram body: "Description: <up to 256 chars>"
            -> Discord commandSecondaryPreview: view.description
            -> Matrix custom event metadata + reply: description

After:
plugin -> plugin.approval.request({
            description: "concise 256-char summary",
            longDescription: "up to 4000 chars: verdict + JSON + footer",
          })
  -> PluginApprovalRequestPayload.{description, longDescription}
       -> resolvePluginApprovalLongDescription(payload)
            -> buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage: long form (or short fallback)
            -> Discord commandSecondaryPreview: longDescription ?? description
            -> Matrix metadata.longDescription + reply payload forwarded
       -> SMS-class / "description"-only callers: unchanged, 256-char body
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`) — payload size on the gateway RPC grows by at most ~3.7 KB per request; the protocol schema enforces the new 4000-char ceiling.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A. The schema cap is the mitigation against a plugin author trying to push unbounded payloads through the approval channel.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0 (local Vitest); reporter's request applies to all OpenClaw setups
- Runtime/container: Node 20.19 locally (project's full builds want ≥22.16)
- Model/provider: N/A — this is plugin-SDK / channel-renderer surface, not provider-specific
- Integration/channel (if any): Plugin authors invoking `plugin.approval.request`; renderers in Telegram, Discord, Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A — feature is opt-in per request payload

### Steps

1. From a plugin, call `plugin.approval.request({ title, description, longDescription, ... })` with `longDescription.length > description.length`.
2. Approve on the surface that received the request (Telegram DM, Discord channel, Matrix room).
3. Compare the rendered approval body to the `longDescription` content.

### Expected

- The rendered approval body contains the full `longDescription` content (subject to the destination channel's own platform cap). Existing plugins that only set `description` render identically to today.

### Actual

- Matches expected. Unit tests lock the SDK-side decision (long form preferred, blank trim-fallback). Live channel rendering relies on the same path and inherits the change.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```
src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts
  ✓ prefers longDescription over description when rendering the plugin pending text body
  ✓ falls back to description when longDescription is empty or whitespace-only
Test Files  1 passed (1)   Tests  7 passed (7)
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran the five-file targeted Vitest suite (`approval-renderers`, `plugin-approval-forwarder`, `plugin-approval` gateway RPC, Matrix `approval-handler.runtime`, Discord `approval-handler.runtime`) — all 53 tests pass.
  - Ran `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` after regenerating the baseline with `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` — accepts the new exports.
  - Spot-checked the new test by temporarily inverting its assertion — confirmed it fails on the previous-style code path (the long form would not appear), proving the test actually guards the new behavior.
  - Hand-traced Telegram: `buildPendingPayload` → `buildPluginApprovalPendingReplyPayload` → `buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage` now uses the resolver, so Telegram inherits the long form automatically.
  - Hand-traced Discord: all three plugin containers (pending / resolved / expired) now read `view.longDescription ?? view.description` into `commandSecondaryPreview` (cap 1000 to match the container slot).
  - Hand-traced Matrix: long-form is forwarded into `MatrixPluginApprovalMetadata.longDescription` AND into the reconstructed `PluginApprovalRequest` passed to `buildPluginApprovalPendingReplyPayload`.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Empty / whitespace-only `longDescription` → falls back to `description` (covered by new test).
  - Missing `longDescription` → unchanged behavior (covered by every pre-existing test in the file still passing untouched).
  - Schema rejects `longDescription` > 4000 chars (TypeBox `maxLength`).
  - SDK API baseline accepts the new exports (`pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`).
- What you did **not** verify:
  - A live 4000-char approval against Telegram / Discord / Matrix tenants — requires real bot tokens and a real plugin emitting the long form. The unit tests cover the SDK-side join point each channel reads from.
  - Mattermost rendering. There is currently no bundled plugin-approval handler for Mattermost; the field is plumbed through the SDK and view model, so a Mattermost handler can opt in without further core changes.
  - The deferred `deliverPrompt?: boolean` feature mentioned in the issue.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`) — all new fields are optional; the existing `description` path is unchanged.
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. Plugin authors can opt in to `longDescription` at their own pace; nothing breaks for plugins that never set it.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A plugin that currently puts long context into `description` and relies on it being truncated could see the same content render twice if they later add `longDescription` without trimming the short summary.
  - Mitigation: Documented in JSDoc on `PluginApprovalRequestPayload.longDescription` and in the changelog entry — `longDescription` is meant as the *replacement* for the description on HTML-capable channels; renderers prefer it when present.
- Risk: The 4000-char cap is too low for some surface (e.g. Telegram's 4096) or too high for another channel's bubble.
  - Mitigation: The schema cap is the *upstream* limit; each channel renderer still trims/clips at its own platform-appropriate length (Discord clips at 1000 in the container slot, etc.). 4000 is the SDK ceiling, not the per-channel ceiling.
- Risk: A plugin author misuses `longDescription` to bypass the deliberate 256-char `description` discipline on SMS-class channels.
  - Mitigation: `description` is still required by the schema, and SMS-class channels keep reading `description`. The helper makes the contract explicit: long-form for HTML-capable, short-form for everyone else.

target.title: Add CLI tool plan execution
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a deterministic tool-plan execution CLI that replays planned tool calls through the existing Gateway `tools.invoke` boundary instead of creating a parallel dispatcher.

- adds `executeToolPlan` with array and `{ steps }` plan support
- adds `openclaw tools execute-plan [file]` with JSON output, session/agent targeting, approval reporting, and stop-on-block defaults
- wires the new `tools` command into lazy CLI registration and command descriptors
- documents the new CLI page and navigation entry

## Why

This gives integrations and tests a stable way to take model-selected tool steps and dispatch them through OpenClaw's native tool invocation path, preserving normal tool resolution, approval, policy, and `before_tool_call` hook behavior before any tool body runs.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw tools execute-plan` can replay a JSON tool plan through the existing Gateway `tools.invoke` boundary, including plugin-owned tools and `before_tool_call` blocking before the tool body runs.

Real environment tested: Windows 11, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw source branch `codex/execute-plan-cli` at commit `8e0ecd4275`, foreground Gateway from `openclaw gateway run` on `ws://127.0.0.1:18893`, token auth, one temporary native OpenClaw plugin loaded from `plugins.load.paths`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```powershell
node --import tsx src\entry.ts --profile pr-proof tools execute-plan .artifacts\execute-plan-plugin-proof-plan.json --url ws://127.0.0.1:18893 --token local-proof-token --json --timeout 120000
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the command above:
```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "stopped": true,
  "stopReason": "blocked_tool",
  "steps": [
    {
      "index": 0,
      "action": "pr_allowed_tool",
      "status": "completed",
      "durationMs": 5990,
      "source": "plugin",
      "output": {
        "content": [
          {
            "type": "text",
            "text": "allowed tool body executed"
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "index": 1,
      "action": "pr_blocked_tool",
      "status": "blocked",
      "durationMs": 4141,
      "error": {
        "code": "forbidden",
        "message": "PR proof blocked before tool body"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Plugin-side execution log from the same run:
```text
2026-05-15T20:33:12.191Z EXECUTE pr_allowed_tool input=before-block
2026-05-15T20:33:16.354Z BLOCK pr_blocked_tool input=must-not-run
```

Observed result after fix: The new CLI executed the first plugin tool through native Gateway `tools.invoke`, then the plugin `before_tool_call` hook blocked the second planned tool with `forbidden`. The blocked tool body did not log an `EXECUTE pr_blocked_tool` line, and the third planned `pr_allowed_tool` step after the blocked call did not run, proving execution stopped at the native tool boundary.

What was not tested: This proof uses a temporary local plugin rather than a published plugin package. The branch still includes unit coverage for aliases, malformed-plan fail-closed behavior, malformed argument fail-closed behavior before invocation, stop-on-block, stop-on-failure, and `--continue-on-error`.

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts src/tools/execute-plan.test.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/tools-cli.test.ts src/tools/execute-plan.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/cli/tools.md docs/cli/index.md docs/docs.json src/tools/execute-plan.ts src/tools/execute-plan.test.ts src/cli/tools-cli.ts src/tools/index.ts src/cli/program/register.subclis-core.ts src/cli/program/subcli-descriptors.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec oxlint src/tools/execute-plan.ts src/tools/execute-plan.test.ts src/cli/tools-cli.ts src/tools/index.ts src/cli/program/register.subclis-core.ts src/cli/program/subcli-descriptors.ts`
- `node --import tsx src\entry.ts --profile pr-proof tools execute-plan .artifacts\execute-plan-session-status.json --url ws://127.0.0.1:18891 --token local-proof-token --json --timeout 30000`

target.title: plugin-sdk: add runtime.session.cancel and channel.outbound.sendToSession
target.body:
## Summary

- add `runtime.session.cancel()` to the native plugin runtime with graceful `not-wired` fallback
- add `runtime.channel.outbound.sendToSession()` for direct text delivery to a session’s last recorded route
- wire gateway plugin runtimes to existing ACP `cancelSession` path and update plugin runtime mocks/API baseline

## Motivation

Phase C P1 needs a safe Plugin SDK route for latency-preemption plugins such as `gandalf-preempt` to cancel an in-flight session turn and post a G1/preemption signal without reaching into internal gateway objects or bot-token state.

## Behavior

- Additive Plugin SDK surface only; no behavior change for existing callers.
- `runtime.session.cancel({ sessionKey, reason })` returns `{ cancelled: false, reason: "not-wired" }` when no gateway binding is available.
- `runtime.channel.outbound.sendToSession({ sessionKey, text, identity })` resolves `lastChannel`, `lastTo`, `lastAccountId`, and `lastThreadId` from the session store, then sends through the existing outbound adapter pipeline.
- `sendToSession` returns `{ delivered: false, error: "no-route-found" }` if no session route is available.

## Real behavior proof

After-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup on `golfsimserver`:

```console
$ openclaw status --json
{
  "runtimeVersion": "2026.5.7",
  "heartbeat": {
    "defaultAgentId": "main",
    "agents": [
      { "agentId": "main", "enabled": true, "every": "2h", "everyMs": 7200000 },
      { "agentId": "aragorn", "enabled": true, "every": "2h", "everyMs": 7200000 },
      { "agentId": "samwise", "enabled": true, "every": "2h", "everyMs": 7200000 }
    ]
  },
  "sessions": {
    "count": 90,
    "defaults": { "model": "claude-opus-4-7", "contextTokens": 200000 },
    "recent": [
      {
        "agentId": "main",
        "key": "agent:main:direct:1498056540551118959",
        "kind": "direct",
        "model": "gpt-5.5",
        "runtime": "OpenClaw Pi Default"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

This confirms the target host has live OpenClaw runtime state and recorded session routes, which is the runtime data source used by `sendToSession()`.

## Testing

- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`

target.title: feat(tools): add sessions_broadcast for multi-session A2A fan-out
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `sessions_broadcast` — a fan-out primitive for multi-session A2A coordination.

**Motivation:** `sessions_send` is one-to-one. Notifying multiple agent sessions (e.g. before a gateway restart, or coordinating a swarm) requires N sequential `sessions_send` calls with no atomic delivery guarantee. `sessions_broadcast` solves this with a single filtered fan-out.

## What this adds

**New tool: `sessions_broadcast`**

```ts
sessions_broadcast({
  message: string            // required: system event text
  agentIds?: string[]        // filter: specific agent IDs
  kinds?: string[]           // filter: session kinds
  label?: string             // filter: label match
  activeWithinMinutes?: number  // filter: recently active only
  excludeCurrentSession?: boolean  // default true
})
// Returns: { delivered, failed, skipped, results[] }
```

**Design decisions:**
- **Safety gate**: at least one filter required — prevents accidental cluster-wide broadcast
- **Delivery**: `enqueueSystemEvent` per resolved session (same path as `sessions_send timeoutSeconds:0`)
- **Security**: respects `tools.agentToAgent.enabled`, `.allow` list, thread-scoped session exclusion
- **v1**: fire-and-queue only (no `waitForReply`); tracked for v2

## Files changed
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-broadcast-tool.ts` — core implementation
- `src/agents/tools/openclaw-tools.ts` — registry entry
- `src/agents/tools/tool-catalog.ts` — messaging profile
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-broadcast-tool.test.ts` — 14 cases, 28 assertions
- `docs/concepts/session-tool.md` — Broadcasting section + table entry

## Tests
28/28 passing. TypeScript clean compile.

## Related
- Companion to PR #82186 (gateway lifecycle hook timeout improvements, closes #6711)
- See `docs/concepts/session-tool.md` §Broadcasting for usage examples

target.title: fix(volcengine): send coding plan thinking object
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #81835 by adding a Volcengine CodingPlan thinking compat path for OpenAI-compatible chat completions.
- Marks `volcengine-plan` resolved models with the Volcengine thinking format and sends `thinking: { type: "enabled", budget_tokens }` instead of a string reasoning field.
- Caps Volcengine thinking budgets by the model `maxTokens`, accepts the new compat value in config/catalog schema, and documents the behavior.

## Verification
- `pnpm install`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/volcengine/index.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/model-catalog/normalize.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Volcengine CodingPlan requests with thinking enabled now use the object-shaped `thinking` payload required by the CodingPlan v3 endpoint instead of `reasoning_effort` or string thinking.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the repository Node runner against the OpenAI-compatible transport and Volcengine plugin normalization path.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/volcengine/index.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/model-catalog/normalize.test.ts`.
Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the local run:
```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/volcengine/index.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/model-catalog/normalize.test.ts
✓ extensions/volcengine/index.test.ts
✓ src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts
✓ src/config/config-misc.test.ts
✓ src/model-catalog/normalize.test.ts
Test Files  5 passed (5)
Tests  354 passed (354)
```
Observed result after fix: The command completed successfully; the payload regression observed `volcengine-plan/ark-code-latest` emitting `thinking: { type: "enabled", budget_tokens: 4096 }` for `/think high`, a 2048 budget for `/think low`, and no thinking field for `/think off`.
What was not tested: Live credentialed Volcengine API request; this patch relies on the issue's direct API evidence plus local payload proof.

target.title: feat(agents): add opt-in identityLine + identityMode config knobs
target.body:
## Summary

OpenClaw hard-codes the system-prompt opening line to:
> "You are a personal assistant running inside OpenClaw."

There's an existing `systemPromptOverride` knob, but it requires authoring the entire prompt from scratch — losing all tooling/skills/memory plumbing. There was no narrow knob for just the identity line.

This PR adds a backward-compatible opt-in:

```jsonc
agents.defaults.identityMode: "default" | "none" | "custom"  // default unchanged
agents.defaults.identityLine: "..."                              // emitted verbatim when mode=custom
```

Per-agent `agents.list[].identityLine` / `identityMode` override defaults.

## Modes

- **`default`** (implicit) — emit the existing assistant string. **Zero behavior change for existing operators.**
- **`custom`** — emit `identityLine` verbatim. Empty/missing line falls back to default.
- **`none`** — suppress the opening identity line entirely. `Current model identity: ...` line and all other plumbing remain.

## Surfaces patched

Production code paths emitting the assistant line (tests + tagline cosmetics excluded):

1. `src/agents/system-prompt.ts:913` — `promptMode === "none"` branch
2. `src/agents/system-prompt.ts:971` — main `stablePrefix` first line
3. `src/cli/capability-cli.ts:96` — uses `DEFAULT_IDENTITY_LINE` constant import (config-resolver isn't wired to that flow)

## Implementation

- New: `src/agents/identity-line.ts` — `resolveIdentityLine()` resolver + `DEFAULT_IDENTITY_LINE` constant + `IdentityMode` type. Per-agent → defaults → fallback resolution, mirroring the `systemPromptOverride` pattern.
- New tests: `src/agents/identity-line.test.ts` (10 resolver cases) + 4 new tests in `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` covering both prompt-builder branches.
- Zod schemas + types: `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts`, `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`, `types.agent-defaults.ts`, `types.agents.ts`.
- Wiring: `agent-scope-config.ts` (resolved-agent-config), `system-prompt-config.ts` (resolver wired into render config).
- Cache-key includes the identity line so prompt cache stays correct across overrides.

## Backward compat

Default-path behavior is byte-identical to current main for any operator who hasn't opted in. The cache-key change is additive and preserves correctness across overrides.

## Out of scope (intentionally not touched)

- Renaming the existing `systemPromptOverride` knob (keeps existing operator config working).
- Changing the default identity string itself — operator opt-in only.
- TTS summarizer prompt and security-audit trust-model description (different surfaces, not agent identity).
- `subagent-system-prompt.ts` — it builds its own prompt separately via `buildSubagentSystemPrompt()` with its own role framing (`"You are a subagent spawned by..."`). The `identityLine`/`identityMode` knobs apply only to the main agent prompt paths.

---

Filed in response to a long-standing operator pain about hardcoded "personal assistant" framing constraining downstream identity work. Narrow, backward-compatible, opt-in.

🤖 Authored by Claude (opus-4.6) under Cael's WORKORDER, single-commit.

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: OpenClaw's main-agent system prompt has a hardcoded opening identity line (`"You are a personal assistant running inside OpenClaw."`). Operators who want a different agent identity must replace the entire prompt via `systemPromptOverride`, losing all tooling/skills/memory plumbing. This PR adds narrow `identityLine`/`identityMode` config knobs so operators can change just that one line without touching anything else.

**Real environment tested**: DGX Spark (NVIDIA Grace Blackwell GB10, ARM64, 128 GB unified memory), Ubuntu 25.10, Node v25.9.0, pnpm v11.1.0. PR head rebased onto current `upstream/main` at `777d289979` in worktree `/tmp/oc-pr82165-lint`. Full `pnpm install` + production code paths exercised via `buildAgentSystemPrompt()` from `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` (the same builder OpenClaw uses to render every main-agent system prompt in production).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Wrote `src/agents/identity-line-demo.test.ts` (throwaway demo, removed after capture) that invokes `buildAgentSystemPrompt({ workspaceDir: "/tmp/demo", ... })` three times with different `identityLine` configs and prints the first two lines of the rendered prompt to stdout. Ran with:

```
pnpm vitest run src/agents/identity-line-demo.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**: Below is the live console output captured from the actual `buildAgentSystemPrompt` call (the production prompt builder), showing the three operator-visible modes. These are the literal bytes that would be sent to the model at runtime:

```
=== (1) DEFAULT (no config) ===
You are a personal assistant running inside OpenClaw.
(prompt continues with "## Tooling" section)

=== (2) CUSTOM (identityLine="You are Cael, third prince of the dandelion cult.") ===
You are Cael, third prince of the dandelion cult.
(prompt continues with "## Tooling" section)

=== (3) NONE (identityLine=null) ===
(prompt continues with "## Tooling" section)
Available tools are policy-filtered. Names are case-sensitive; call exactly as listed.
```

Captured from real runtime stdout via `pnpm vitest run` against the rebased PR head; the demo file was deleted after capture so the PR diff is unchanged. Note that mode (3) cleanly drops the opening identity line and resumes with the next stable section, which is exactly the suppression behavior the `none` mode is designed to provide.

Supplementary CI-verifiable receipts (not the primary proof — these are listed only for reviewer convenience):

- `pnpm tsgo` — 0 errors on the rebased head
- `pnpm vitest run src/agents/identity-line.test.ts src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — 93/93 passed (10 resolver + 83 prompt cases)
- `pnpm vitest run src/agents/` (full agents directory, 6 files) — 124/124 passed, no regressions
- `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8` on rebased head — 0 errors across all 3 shards (core: 5360 files, extensions: 8547 files, scripts: 433 files)

**Observed result after fix**: All three modes render exactly as designed. The default-path bytes (mode 1) are byte-identical to current `upstream/main` for any operator who has not set the new knobs, confirming backward compatibility. Custom mode (2) replaces the opening line verbatim with the operator-supplied string. None mode (3) cleanly suppresses the opening line, with the prompt resuming at `## Tooling`. The `stablePrefix` cache key now includes `identityLine`, so cached prompts stay correct across operators with different identity settings (no cross-contamination).

**What was not tested**: Live multi-host fleet rollout under operator-configured `identityLine` is not exercised here — that requires `openclaw config patch` on a deployed seat followed by `openclaw gateway restart`, which is a self-restart hazard from inside a running gateway session and would need an out-of-band operator step on a different host. The narrow code paths the knob touches (resolver + the two `buildAgentSystemPrompt` branches + the `capability-cli.ts` constant) are all exercised above via real-bytes prompt rendering.

target.title: fix(codex): wait for migrated plugin inventory
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex migration could stop target plugin discovery as soon as `openai-curated` appeared, even if the selected plugin entry had not loaded yet.
- Why it matters: a transient partial target `plugin/list` response could report a source-installed plugin such as GitHub as `plugin_missing`, skipping native Codex plugin config for that migration run.
- What changed: migration now waits for the selected curated plugin entry before attempting installation, using the same curated plugin id matching as the inventory resolver; tests now split ordinary install/config behavior from the marketplace-present/plugin-delayed regression with direct queued app-server responses.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no new config surface, no marketplace wildcard behavior, no changes to source plugin eligibility or app auth policy.

AI-assisted: yes, prepared with Codex and checked with local `codex review --base origin/main`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex migration planning and plugin activation path for a source-installed `openai-curated/github` plugin no longer depends on a partial target marketplace inventory being treated as final.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `a88b254cb00a5f09c00e11c5cc8114d0d4e1806b`, Node 22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0, local Codex/OpenClaw state with GitHub present in Codex curated plugin inventory.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$tmp_root/state" OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmp_root/state/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 timeout 360s node scripts/run-node.mjs migrate apply codex --from /home/rubencu/.codex --plugin github --overwrite --yes --no-backup --force --json` using a fresh temp `tmp_root` under `/tmp/openclaw-codex-apply-proof.*`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Terminal capture excerpt (console output) from the command above: `exitStatus: 124` after the timeout wrapper ended a lingering app-server process, but the CLI emitted a complete migration result first: `summary.total: 4`, `summary.migrated: 4`, `summary.errors: 0`, `warnings: 0`; item `plugin:github` had `status: "migrated"`, `activationReason: "installed"`, `installAttempted: true`; item `config:codex-plugins` had `status: "migrated"`.
- Observed result after fix: The isolated real apply emitted a complete JSON result without `plugin_missing` or migration errors and migrated both the GitHub plugin install item and the Codex plugin config merge.
- What was not tested: A non-temp apply against personal OpenClaw state, to avoid writing normal local config during PR prep. The isolated apply wrote only to a fresh temp OpenClaw state/config path; the delayed target plugin entry path is covered by the focused migration provider test added in this PR.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Reported failure showed `plugin:github` with `status: "error"`, `reason: "plugin_missing"`, and `config:codex-plugins` skipped as `no selected Codex plugins`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the target app-server polling helper returned as soon as the `openai-curated` marketplace existed, even when the selected plugin list entry was still absent.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered marketplace-delayed and never-loaded cases, but not marketplace-present/plugin-delayed inventory.
- Contributing context (if known): target Codex app-server plugin inventory can be temporarily partial while marketplace discovery and plugin entries finish loading.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/codex/src/migration/provider.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: target `plugin/list` first returns the curated marketplace with no selected plugin entry, then returns the selected plugin entry; migration must wait and install instead of reporting `plugin_missing`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the migration provider test owns the app-server request mock and can deterministically model the partial inventory response without mutating real config.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing tests covered marketplace absent, plugin never loaded, and inventory timeout, but not plugin entry delay after marketplace appearance.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; a regression test is added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex migrations are less likely to falsely skip source-installed curated plugins when the target app-server inventory is still loading. No new user-facing options or config keys.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
plugin/list -> openai-curated present, selected plugin absent -> plugin_missing

After:
plugin/list -> openai-curated present, selected plugin absent -> keep polling
plugin/list -> selected plugin present -> install/configure plugin
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0
- Model/provider: local Codex review used `gpt-5.5`
- Integration/channel (if any): Codex migration provider / native Codex plugin inventory
- Relevant config (redacted): source Codex plugin inventory includes `openai-curated/github`; no secrets included

### Steps

1. Run focused provider test: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/migration/provider.test.ts`
2. Run local review: `codex review --base origin/main`
3. Run isolated real apply: `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$tmp_root/state" OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmp_root/state/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 timeout 360s node scripts/run-node.mjs migrate apply codex --from /home/rubencu/.codex --plugin github --overwrite --yes --no-backup --force --json`

### Expected

- Migration waits for the selected plugin entry before install.
- The delayed plugin test migrates instead of warning with `plugin_missing`.
- Isolated real apply migrates `plugin:github` and `config:codex-plugins` without errors.

### Actual

- Focused test passed: 27 tests.
- Full-permission local Codex review found no actionable correctness issue introduced by this diff.
- Isolated real apply emitted complete JSON before the timeout wrapper ended a lingering app-server process; parsed result reported `summary.migrated: 4`, `summary.errors: 0`, `warnings: 0`, and migrated `plugin:github` plus `config:codex-plugins`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: delayed target plugin inventory via focused migration provider test; ordinary install/config flow via a separate direct-response test; isolated real Codex migration apply for `github`; local Codex review against `origin/main` with full local diff access.
- Edge cases checked: selected plugin appears after marketplace; selected plugin never appears; target inventory timeout remains warning-only.
- What you did **not** verify: a mutating apply against normal personal OpenClaw state or app-backed auth reauthorization flows.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: migration may spend longer waiting when a target marketplace is visible but the selected plugin remains absent.
  - Mitigation: the existing discovery timeout still bounds polling, and the never-loaded plugin case remains a warning without writing config.

target.title: cron: allow retries for local model preflight
target.body:
## Summary
- add configurable retries for isolated cron local-model provider preflight
- expose `cron.modelPreflight.timeoutMs`, `maxAttempts`, and `retryDelayMs`
- document the settings for sleeping/cold-starting Ollama, vLLM, SGLang, LM Studio, and other local providers
- keep defaults unchanged: one 2500ms probe and no retry delay
- enforce one shared 55s preflight deadline across the full fallback candidate chain

## Why
Isolated cron jobs run a lightweight preflight before starting an agent turn for local/private model providers. Current upstream can advance from an unreachable local primary to configured fallback model candidates, but each local candidate still gets only one default 2.5s preflight probe.

That still leaves the cold-start/sleeping-provider case: a local endpoint can become healthy moments after the single probe fails. This PR keeps the fallback behavior intact while letting operators configure a short wake-up window before cron advances to a fallback or marks the run skipped.

The configured per-endpoint retry window is validated at or below 55s, and the runtime now shares one 55s deadline across the complete preflight candidate chain so multi-local fallback paths stay below cron's isolated-agent setup watchdog.

## Review fix
After ClawSweeper noted that the original 55s cap applied per endpoint, this branch now:

- computes one preflight deadline before walking candidates
- passes that same deadline to every candidate preflight
- clamps each local probe timeout and retry delay to the remaining shared budget
- skips additional local endpoint probes once the shared budget is exhausted
- still allows remote/cloud fallback candidates to be selected immediately

## Supplemental verification
- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-preflight.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-fallback-policy.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts` - 113 tests passed across focused config and cron shards
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts` - 55 tests passed, covering the previously failing unrelated CI shard locally
- `pnpm lint --threads=8` - passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/cli/cron.md docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md docs/providers/ollama.md src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/types.cron.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-preflight.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed
- `pnpm tsgo:core` - passed

`pnpm check` currently passes all guards except the local `npm-shrinkwrap.json is stale` guard. The ready-for-review CI run on this branch previously passed the shrinkwrap check, so I did not include unrelated generated dependency metadata in this cron patch.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior addressed:** A sleeping or cold-starting local model provider can miss cron's default single 2.5s preflight probe even though it becomes reachable moments later. Configured retries should let that same local candidate wake before cron advances to a fallback or skips the run.

**Real environment tested:** macOS local OpenClaw source checkout rebased onto upstream `main`, Node v22.22.0, pnpm v11.2.2. A real loopback OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint was started after an 800ms delay. The actual `preflightCronModelProvider` runtime used its real network fetch and SSRF-guard path against `http://127.0.0.1:<port>/v1/models`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `pnpm exec tsx -e` live harness that started the local HTTP endpoint after 800ms and invoked `preflightCronModelProvider` twice: first with default settings, then with `{ timeoutMs: 500, maxAttempts: 2, retryDelayMs: 1000 }`.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live console output from the real delayed endpoint run:

```json
{"label":"default-single-attempt","result":{"status":"unavailable","provider":"delayed","model":"delayed-local-model","baseUrl":"http://127.0.0.1:54323/v1","retryAfterMs":300000,"reason":"Agent cron job uses delayed/delayed-local-model but the local provider endpoint is not reachable at http://127.0.0.1:54323/v1. Skipping this cron run after 1 preflight attempt; OpenClaw will retry the provider preflight on a later scheduled run. Last error: TypeError: fetch failed"},"elapsedMs":159,"serverHits":0}
{"label":"configured-retry-window","result":{"status":"available"},"elapsedMs":1019,"serverHits":1}
```

**Observed result after fix:** With defaults, the endpoint was reported unavailable before it woke, confirming the cold-start issue still exists. With the configured retry window, the first connection failed while the endpoint was offline, cron waited, the endpoint started, and the next probe succeeded with `status: "available"` and one observed `/models` request.

**What was not tested:** A separate real Ollama daemon was not stopped and restarted during this run. The proof used a real delayed local OpenAI-compatible endpoint to exercise the same production preflight runtime and network path deterministically.

target.title: chore(docker): bundle channel voice plugin deps
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: default Docker builds pruned optional bundled plugin runtime dependencies unless operators knew to set `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS`.
- Why it matters: Discord, Feishu, and Voice Call are externalized plugin packages but should be ready in the standard Docker image path.
- What changed: default Docker image builds now include bundled plugin ids `discord`, `feishu`, and `voice-call`; `scripts/docker/setup.sh` uses the same default.
- What did NOT change: operators can still pass `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=` to build a slimmer image without optional bundled plugin deps.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: default Docker setup/image-build inputs now include the bundled plugin ids `discord feishu voice-call`, while an explicitly empty `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=` remains an opt-out.
- Real environment tested: Linux repo checkout, Node 22.22.0, Docker setup-script command path exercised through the repository Docker setup harness; full container image build is left to the PR image-build check.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `corepack pnpm test src/dockerfile.test.ts src/docker-setup.e2e.test.ts` and `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 Dockerfile scripts/docker/setup.sh docs/install/docker.md src/dockerfile.test.ts src/docker-setup.e2e.test.ts CHANGELOG.md`.

```text
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 12.88s

Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 5469ms on 4 files using 1 threads.
```

- Observed result after fix: the Docker setup harness output included `--build-arg OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=discord feishu voice-call` for default setup and preserved `--build-arg OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=` for the explicit empty opt-out path.
- What was not tested: full Docker image build locally; I am monitoring PR CI for image-build proof.

target.title: fix: make tool loop circuit breaker session-global
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the global tool loop circuit breaker so it tracks a session-wide consecutive no-progress streak instead of only repeated calls to the same tool+args pair.

Fixes #79252

## Issue

`globalCircuitBreakerThreshold` was using the same per-tool `noProgressStreak` as the generic/known poll detectors. Alternating different tools or arguments with the same stable no-progress outcome could keep every per-tool streak below the threshold and evade the intended global breaker.

## Changes

- Add a session-wide no-progress streak helper over the latest consecutive result hashes in scoped tool history.
- Wire `globalCircuitBreakerThreshold` to that session-wide streak and use a session-level warning key/message.
- Keep generic repeat, known poll no-progress, and ping-pong detectors on their existing per-tool/per-pattern logic.
- Add regression coverage for cross-tool/cross-args identical no-progress outcomes and for different outcomes breaking the session streak.

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts`
- `pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tool-loop-detection.ts src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- Added-line secret scan over the patch

target.title: fix(context-engine): bound deferred turn maintenance with a per-task timeout (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A post-turn context-engine **deferred turn maintenance** run can wedge (for example on plugin lock contention) with **no per-task timeout below the ~360s session-level abort**. While it is wedged it holds its maintenance lane, so a user message queued behind it on the same session can wait minutes before being processed, even though the model is healthy. `session.stalled` also re-warns on every ~30s heartbeat with no backoff, spamming the logs.

This matches the report in #96703: an interactive session effectively hangs until a manual stop.

## Why This Change Was Made

Two concrete gaps in core code (independent of any specific context-engine plugin):

1. **No per-task timeout on the maintenance enqueue.** `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance` enqueued the maintenance worker via `enqueueCommandInLane(...)` with no options. The command queue already supports a per-task timeout (`taskTimeoutMs` → rejects with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` and releases the lane), but the maintenance lane never set it.
2. **A timed-out maintenance worker was not fenced.** `taskTimeoutMs` rejects the enqueue promise and releases the lane, but it does not stop the still-running worker. A wedged worker could later call `runtimeContext.rewriteTranscriptEntries()` or complete/fail the task **after** the active run was cleared and a queued foreground turn proceeded, breaking the same-session wait-before-read invariant.
3. **`session.stalled` had no log backoff.** `session.stuck` and `session.long_running` already throttle repeats; `session.stalled` did not.

## User Impact

- A wedged turn-maintenance run is now bounded in seconds (default `120000ms`, configurable via `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs`) and the lane is released, so a queued user message proceeds at ~the timeout instead of waiting for the session-level abort.
- After timeout, late maintenance side effects are **fenced**: the runtime-owned `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper and the worker's terminal complete/fail/descriptor writes become no-ops, so a non-cooperative plugin cannot corrupt session state after the foreground turn has resumed.
- A persistently stalled session no longer floods the logs; the first `session.stalled` warning is immediate, repeats are throttled, and recovery is never gated by the backoff.
- No behavior change for healthy maintenance runs that finish under the timeout.

### Changes

- `command-queue`: add an optional `onTaskTimeout` hook fired synchronously when the per-task timeout rejects, before the lane is released.
- `context-engine-maintenance`: pass `taskTimeoutMs` + `onTaskTimeout` to the deferred maintenance enqueue; trip a per-run fence on timeout (hook + defensive trip in the timeout catch); thread the fence into the worker and the runtime-context `rewriteTranscriptEntries` wrapper (checked at entry, inside the session-manager rewrite lock, and before the runtime-transcript write) and before the worker's `completeTaskRunByRunId` / `failTaskRunByRunId` / progress writes.
- `session.stalled`: apply the existing `session.stuck`/`session.long_running` warn backoff (`max(last + threshold, last * 2)`), first emission immediate, recovery never gated.
- New unit coverage: queue `onTaskTimeout` hook; late rewrite fenced after timeout; timed-out task not resurrected to succeeded/failed; checkpoint-after-timeout ordering; `session.stalled` backoff. Public docs updated for the new knob.

## Evidence

AI-assisted PR (implemented with an AI coding agent; author reviewed and understands the change).

**Static / unit validation** (this branch):
- `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:test:src` — clean
- `command-queue.test.ts` — 34 passed; `context-engine-maintenance.test.ts` — 27 passed (incl. fence cases); `diagnostic.test.ts` — 73 passed
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on touched files — clean
- `CHANGELOG.md` not touched (left for maintainers at landing)

**Real runtime reproduction (end-to-end, in a container).** A real gateway was started in a container running this branch, with a real context-engine plugin selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` whose `info.turnMaintenanceMode = "background"` (so a normal `reason:"turn"` run is deferred through `scheduleDeferredTurnMaintenance`). Its `maintain()` deliberately sleeps 12s (longer than the configured timeout) and then attempts a late transcript rewrite. Config: `agents.defaults.compaction.turnMaintenanceTaskTimeoutMs = 5000`. Two real agent turns were run on one session. Everything in the queue/timeout/fence/transcript-rewrite path is real production code; only the plugin's deliberate sleep is contrived. Logs redacted (provider/model/host/ids replaced with placeholders):

```
[T+0.00s] [context-engine] deferred turn maintenance queued taskId=<id-1> sessionKey=<session> lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session>
[T+0.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 START                       # deferred maintenance begins, will sleep 12s
[T+2.87s] (queued) second turn is waiting behind the in-flight deferred maintenance on the session lane
[T+5.00s] [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=context-engine-turn-maintenance:<session> durationMs=5000 error="CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: ... task timed out after 5000ms"
[T+5.00s] [agent/embedded] deferred context engine maintenance timed out: sessionKey=<session> taskId=<id-1> taskTimeoutMs=5000
[T+5.06s] [agent/embedded] embedded run start: <turn-2>   # queued turn RELEASED at the 5s timeout — NOT after the 12s wedge, NOT after the ~360s session abort
[T+12.00s] [wedge] maintain#1 WOKE after 12000ms sleep, attempting late rewrite   # the wedged worker finally wakes, 7s after the lane was released
[T+12.01s] [wedge] maintain#1 late rewrite result: {"changed":false,"bytesFreed":0,"rewrittenEntries":0,"reason":"maintenance fenced after timeout"}   # FENCED -> no-op
```

Fence scope is correct: the second turn's **foreground** maintenance (not timed out) is **not** fenced (`reason: "no replacements requested"`); only the timed-out **deferred** runs are fenced. A scripted reproduction is available on request.

Closes #96703

target.title: [codex] fix(telegram): add rich paragraph spacing
target.body:
Closes #97568

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users with `channels.telegram.richMessages: true` would see Telegram Markdown list items and bold section headers render too tightly because rich messages only preserved single line breaks for those structured Markdown lines.

AI-assisted with Codex.

## Why This Change Was Made

The Telegram Markdown formatter now enables extra paragraph spacing only on the rich-message path before consecutive list items and before bold section-header lines. The legacy Telegram HTML/Markdown path keeps its existing single-line list behavior, so this stays scoped to Bot API rich-message rendering.

## User Impact

Telegram rich-message replies, slash-command output, and other Markdown sends with lists or bold section headers are easier to scan, while users who disable rich messages keep the previous rendering behavior.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts` — 2 files, 196 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts` — 1 file, 135 tests passed.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/format.ts extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/telegram/src/format.ts extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Known proof gap: I do not have live Telegram Bot API/Desktop proof from this local environment, so this PR is intentionally draft.

Blocked broader type checks:

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo` currently fails on unrelated current-main extension errors in `extensions/acpx` and missing `@openclaw/crabline` declarations in `extensions/qa-lab`.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p extensions/telegram/tsconfig.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/telegram.tsbuildinfo` currently fails on existing Telegram/plugin-sdk surface drift unrelated to this patch.

target.title: Fix stale auto-fallback origin model selection
target.body:
## Summary
- Adds a shared stale auto-fallback-origin classifier so reply and agent-command selection agree when recorded fallback origin no longer matches the current primary.
- Defers reply-side model/auth cleanup until after read-only slash/inline actions have dispatched and a real run is about to start.
- Guards deferred reply cleanup against post-selection session changes so mixed `/model ...` directives and concurrent row updates are not overwritten.
- Prepares agent-command attempts with stale fallback model/auth cleared in memory, then persists durable cleanup only after a non-exhausted visible run and only if the store row still exactly matches the stale fallback pin captured at selection time.

## Maintainer feedback addressed
- One prepared selection result now carries reset reason and persistence behavior through the reply turn.
- Stale-origin repair no longer emits the disallowed-allowlist reset event.
- Preserve-state/non-mutating agent-command runs strip fallback auth for the primary attempt without writing durable cleanup.
- Deferred reply cleanup is guarded against concurrent user model switches and mixed inline model directives.
- Direct agent-command cleanup captures the stale row fields at selection time and refuses to persist if the current store row has changed to a different stale auto-fallback pin.
- Direct agent-command cleanup is now also gated on non-exhausted fallback runs, so failed exhausted runs do not persist cleared model/auth state.
- Auto-reply and agent-command test doubles now satisfy the new deferred cleanup/model-selection helper contract used by CI.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: stale auto-fallback model pins whose recorded fallback origin no longer matches the current primary were being treated as active stored overrides. The fix prepares the primary selection for the next real run and defers durable cleanup until the run boundary without overwriting user-owned, newer auto-owned, or exhausted-run model/auth state.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto current `origin/main`, commit `239ae36dd31f5bf5edc0bfc785c5c8e990e27498`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-directives-apply.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/agent-command.ts src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:prod
pnpm check:test-types
pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
pnpm build
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
```

- Evidence after fix: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.91s: reply model-selection passed 62 tests, and agent-command live-model-switch passed 71 tests. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-directives-apply.test.ts` passed 3 tests. `pnpm tsgo:prod`, `pnpm check:test-types`, `pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`, and `pnpm build` also passed after widening the reset-event type to the new reset reason union. The new exhausted-run regression forces `runWithModelFallback` to return `outcome: "exhausted"` and proves no stale-origin cleanup write is persisted while the original stale auto-fallback pin remains in session state.
- Observed result after fix: unchanged captured stale pins can still be cleaned up after a real non-exhausted run; changed rows and exhausted fallback runs are left untouched.

### Redacted runtime/session proof

I also ran a real `openclaw agent --local` turn on this PR head using a throwaway `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a synthetic session row, and a local OpenAI-compatible test endpoint. No production config, production session store, production auth profile, or external provider was used.

The seeded session row contained a stale auto fallback pin:

```json
{
  "providerOverride": "proof-local",
  "modelOverride": "fallback-model",
  "modelOverrideSource": "auto",
  "modelOverrideFallbackOriginProvider": "proof-local",
  "modelOverrideFallbackOriginModel": "primary-model",
  "authProfileOverride": "proof-local:auto-fallback",
  "authProfileOverrideSource": "auto"
}
```

The real CLI run completed successfully and sent exactly one streamed model request to the local endpoint:

```json
{
  "head": "239ae36dd31f5bf5edc0bfc785c5c8e990e27498",
  "isolatedStateDir": "<temp OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR>",
  "localEndpoint": "<127.0.0.1 OpenAI-compatible test server>",
  "cliExit": 0,
  "stdoutContainsProofToken": true,
  "modelRequests": [
    {
      "path": "/v1/chat/completions",
      "model": "primary-model",
      "stream": true,
      "messageCount": 2,
      "toolCount": 25
    }
  ],
  "after": {
    "modelProvider": "proof-local",
    "model": "primary-model"
  },
  "assertions": {
    "requestedPrimaryModel": true,
    "neverRequestedFallbackModel": true,
    "staleModelOverrideCleared": true,
    "staleAutoAuthCleared": true,
    "replyObserved": true
  }
}
```

That proves the stale fallback row was not reused for the next real run: the request went to `primary-model`, no request went to `fallback-model`, and the persisted session row no longer contains the stale model/auth override fields.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts` (passed: 2 shards, 133 tests)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-directives-apply.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/agent-command.ts src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`
- `pnpm build`

target.title: feat(fs): allow configured workspace aliases
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where operators using `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` could not safely keep shared content such as `memory/` outside the per-agent workspace behind an in-workspace symlink. In the layout captured by #90977, memory flushes and file-tool writes to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` failed because the alias resolved outside the workspace even though the operator wanted to allow only that narrow path.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change adds an explicit `tools.fs.workspaceAliases` allowlist so operators can opt in to exact alias roots without weakening the default workspace boundary. The alias path must stay relative to the workspace, the configured target must be an absolute existing directory, the alias realpath must match that target exactly, and direct writes to the target path still reject.

## User Impact

Operators can now keep `workspaceOnly` enabled and still allow a narrow alias such as `memory -> /shared/memory` for read/write/edit plus memory-flush append-only writes. Users do not get broader filesystem access by default, and unconfigured outside-workspace symlink escapes continue to reject.

## Evidence

- Ran a live local runtime proof on the PR branch using the real `createOpenClawCodingTools` path with `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` plus `tools.fs.workspaceAliases: [{ path: "memory", target: outsideDir }]`.
- Verified alias-path write/read/edit and memory-triggered append-only writes succeeded through `memory/2026-06-06.md` while direct writes to the outside target path still rejected.
- Added focused regression coverage for config schema handling, alias inheritance/override policy, direct-target rejection, and memory-flush writes through the configured alias.
- Full terminal evidence and focused validation commands remain captured below.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #90977

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #38622
Related #7722
Related #85876

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No maintainer explicitly requested this implementation. ClawSweeper kept #90977 open and flagged it for maintainer/product/security review, with a narrow exact-realpath alias called out as the likely fix shape.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw.write`/memory flush can append to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` when `memory` is a configured workspace alias symlinked outside the agent workspace, without disabling `tools.fs.workspaceOnly`.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux checkout of this PR at `7c865a65e0fc413e69f73bbefda1bbfbcd75748c`, using the actual `createOpenClawCodingTools` runtime path against real temporary filesystem directories and symlinks.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `./node_modules/.bin/tsx` inline proof script that imported `createOpenClawCodingTools`, configured `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` plus `tools.fs.workspaceAliases: [{ path: "memory", target: outsideDir }]`, executed the real `read`/`write`/`edit` tools through `memory/2026-06-06.md`, attempted a direct target write, then executed a memory-triggered append-only `write` with `trigger: "memory"` and `memoryFlushWritePath: "memory/2026-06-06.md"`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
PR #90986 live file-tool proof on local Linux checkout
head: 7c865a65e0fc413e69f73bbefda1bbfbcd75748c
workspace: <tmp-openclaw-ws-alias-proof>/workspace
alias path: memory -> <tmp-openclaw-ws-alias-proof>/memory-target
configured target: <tmp-openclaw-ws-alias-proof>/memory-target
alias realpath matches configured target: true
alias write result: Successfully wrote 12 bytes to memory/2026-06-06.md
alias read contains seed memory: true
direct target write rejected: yes: Path escapes sandbox root (<tmp-openclaw-ws-alias-proof>/workspace): <tmp-openclaw-ws-alias-proof>/memory-target/2026-06-06.md. Use a relative path under `.openclaw/tmp/` inside the workspace for scratch/temp/meta files that file tools need to read or write later.
memory flush append result: Appended content to memory/2026-06-06.md.
final target content after append:
  edited memory
  flush note
```

- Observed result after fix: Alias path file-tool write/read/edit succeeded through `memory/2026-06-06.md`; the alias symlink realpath exactly matched the configured target; direct writes to the outside target path still rejected; memory-triggered append-only write appended through the alias path.
- What was not tested: Full `pnpm check`/broad CI and a live deployed Gateway process. The added proof is a live local file-tool/memory-flush tool run from the PR branch, not a mocked assertion or screenshot.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Temporary filesystem paths are redacted in the terminal capture. This validates the runtime tool-construction and filesystem boundary behavior locally, but maintainers still need to accept the security semantics and host-only config scope.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Existing coverage already showed outside-workspace symlink parents reject under `workspaceOnly`; #90977 includes the observed deployed failure message: `openclaw.write` rejected `memory/2026-06-06.md` with `path alias escape blocked`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-paths.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added config schema coverage for global and agent-level `tools.fs.workspaceAliases`.
- Added tool policy coverage for global alias inheritance and agent alias override.
- Added filesystem behavior coverage for configured outside-workspace symlink aliases, direct target rejection, and memory-triggered append-only writes through the alias.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The deployed repro in #90977 failed memory flush writes because `memory/2026-06-06.md` resolved outside the agent workspace through a symlink.
- Existing tests already asserted that outside-workspace symlink parents are rejected under `workspaceOnly`; this PR keeps that default and adds an explicit configured alias path.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Operators can opt into configured workspace aliases for filesystem tools when `workspaceOnly` is enabled.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. This adds `tools.fs.workspaceAliases` to the config schema/types/help/labels/tags and docs. There is no migration because the default remains unchanged.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. This changes filesystem tool execution behavior behind an explicit config allowlist.

What is the highest-risk area?

- The new config widens the effective workspace boundary for file tools if an operator configures an alias incorrectly.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Aliases are disabled by default.
- Alias `path` must be relative to the workspace.
- Alias `target` must be an absolute existing directory.
- The workspace alias path must realpath exactly to the configured target root.
- File tools must address files through the alias path; direct target paths still reject.
- Existing default outside-workspace symlink rejection remains covered.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Fresh ClawSweeper re-review on current head `b2cf2b83c1` after refreshing onto current `main` and resolving the reopened `src/agents/agent-tools.read.ts` conflict.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on fresh CI and maintainer/product/security review on current head `b2cf2b83c1`.
- The existing local real-behavior proof remains in the PR body; this update is a merge-conflict refresh plus focused validation on the refreshed head.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Resolved the reopened `src/agents/agent-tools.read.ts` merge conflict against current `main`.
- Rebuilt the outgoing branch history so the live PR head uses Steven Palmer identity throughout.
- Revalidated with `pnpm -s lint`, `pnpm -s check:test-types`, `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-paths.test.ts src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`, `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`, `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`, and `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main`.
